name: Juba Arabic (Árabi Júba)

ancestors.notes: Descends from the 19th-century Common (Sudanese) Pidgin Arabic of the Turco-Egyptian camps; sister of Ki-Nubi (kcn).
Sudanese Arabic is the closest living relative of the camp lexifier that supplied most of the Juba Arabic lexicon (Manfredi 2017).

Bari, Dinka and other Western-Nilotic languages of the southern-Sudanese speaker community: the substrate behind the five-vowel restructuring, the loss of the Arabic guttural series, and implosive ɓ in substrate loanwords (Manfredi 2017; Manfredi & Petrollino 2013).

notes: Juba Arabic / Árabi Júba (ISO 639-3: pga; Glottolog South Sudanese Creole Arabic, suda1237) — an Arabic-based pidgin-creole, the lingua franca of Juba and much of South Sudan (est. hundreds of thousands of L1 and several million L2 speakers). KEY PHONOLOGY (APiCS chapter 64, Manfredi & Petrollino 2013): (1) FIVE-VOWEL system a e i o u, no phonemic length and no emphatic/plain vowel split — the Arabic three-quality-plus-length system is collapsed. (2) PHARYNGEALS LOST: Arabic ħ and ʕ have no reflex in the consonant inventory (twenty phonemic segments, no pharyngeal column); ʕ is generally dropped and ħ falls together with h. (3) EMPHATICS LOST: the emphatic series tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ is de-emphaticised to plain t d s z. (4) Arabic /x/ has no stable native slot: acrolectally it surfaces as [k] (kámsa < Arabic xamsa 'five'), basilectally as [h] ([hamsa]); /x/ and /ɣ/ are marginal, occurring mainly in Arabic and English loans. (5) STRESS is realised as HIGH PITCH and is lexically and grammatically distinctive: sába 'seven' vs. sabá 'morning'; záman 'when' vs. zamán 'ancient times' — a pitch-accent, not the Arabic weight-based stress. (6) ɓ is an implosive found in substrate loanwords (ɓéko 'to find'). ORTHOGRAPHY: no single official standard; a Latin practical orthography (the one used by Manfredi and in the APiCS chapter, with an acute accent marking the stressed/high-pitch vowel, j = /dʒ/, sh/š = /ʃ/, ny = /ɲ/, ng = /ŋ/, y = /j/) is the emerging written norm, though Arabic script is also used. This spec models that Latin orthography.

sources.author: Manfredi, S. & Petrollino, S.
sources.title: Juba Arabic (Survey chapter and structure dataset)
sources.publisher: In: Michaelis, S.M., Maurer, P., Haspelmath, M. & Huber, M. (eds.), Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures Online, chapter 64, Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology
sources.notes: Open-access, verifiable online. Twenty phonemic consonants (no pharyngeals, no emphatics), five vowels a e i o u, stress = distinctive high pitch. Source of the harvested primary-source gold rows.

sources.author: Manfredi, S.
sources.title: Árabi Júba: un pidgin-créole du Soudan du Sud
sources.notes: Reference grammar. Describes the five-vowel system, the loss of the guttural and emphatic series, the pitch-accent, and the Latin practical orthography.

sources.author: Owens, J.
sources.title: Arabic-based pidgins and creoles
sources.publisher: In: Thomason, S.G. (ed.), Contact Languages: A Wider Perspective, John Benjamins, pp. 125-172
sources.notes: Places Juba Arabic and Ki-Nubi as sister creoles of one 19th-century southern-Sudanese pidgin.

location.notes: Juba, capital of South Sudan and the centre of the Juba Arabic speech community.

stress.notes: Stress is realised phonetically as a HIGH PITCH and is lexically and grammatically distinctive (Manfredi & Petrollino 2013, APiCS ch. 64: sába 'seven' vs. sabá 'morning'; záman 'when' vs. zamán 'ancient times'). Because it is contrastive it cannot be predicted from syllable weight the way Arabic stress can; the practical orthography marks it with an acute accent, so a written acute pins the stressed vowel directly. default_position -2 is only the fallback for unaccented input.
